unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4 of Year 8: Advertising and Persuasion! Every day, you are surrounded by messages designed to grab your attention, change your mind, or convince you to buy something. From billboards towering over busy Mogadishu streets to sponsored posts sliding through your social media feed, advertising is everywhere. But how does it work? What techniques do advertisers use to persuade people? And how can you, as a critical thinker, tell the difference between honest information and clever manipulation?
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the grammar lessons in the Grammar file (Prepositions Preceding Nouns, Passive Voice, Participle Clauses, Reflexive Pronouns, Second Conditional, Comparatives, Compound Adjectives, Reported Speech).
Read the Story file, "Code Name: Bonanza," and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all twelve sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.

visual.alt: Learners analysing advertising, persuasion and audience effect

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A natural ability to do something well.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She has a great aptitude for learning new languages quickly.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'aptitude', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Advertising and Persuasion.

readings.title: Advertising and Persuasion: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
🧭 How to use this unit
This is your main lesson file. It has twelve sections to work through at your own pace.
You also have three companion files: Vocabulary, Grammar (Prepositions Preceding Nouns, Passive Voice, Participle Clauses, Reflexive Pronouns, Second Conditional, Comparatives, Compound Adjectives, and Reported Speech), and the Story "Code Name: Bonanza".
Study a little every day. Use your AI tutor whenever you are stuck, and bring your work to your teacher sessions twice a week.
In this unit, you will explore the language of advertising and persuasion. You will study how advertisers choose their words carefully, how they appeal to emotions, and how they use visual design to make products seem irresistible. You will also examine how persuasive language appears in everyday life — in speeches, debates, product reviews, and even conversations with friends. Understanding these techniques will make you a more attentive reader, a more shrewd consumer, and a more confident communicator.
The grammar focus of this unit is prepositions preceding nouns — small but powerful words like on, in, for, at, and with that appear before nouns to show relationships. You will notice them everywhere in advertising language: items on sale, products in stock, offers valid for a limited time. Alongside prepositions, you will study passive voice, participle clauses, reflexive pronouns, the second conditional, comparatives with "the more / the less", compound adjectives, and reported speech. These grammar tools will help you write more persuasive texts, analyse advertisements critically, and express your own opinions with precision.
You will also read the story "Code Name: Bonanza", set in an East African tech school where student teams compete in an innovation challenge. The story explores how persuasion, branding, and integrity interact in a competitive environment. Through the story, you will see how vocabulary words like cunning, adversary, momentum, and bonanza come alive in context, and you will discover that the most lasting success comes not from clever tricks but from honest effort and ethical strategy.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand passages about advertising techniques, persuasion, and ethical marketing, and discuss their ideas confidently.
Identify and use prepositions preceding nouns correctly in phrases like on sale, in stock, for a limited time, at a discount, and with interest.
Form and use passive voice sentences in the present and past: "The product was launched by the company" and "The advert is designed to attract young people."
Begin sentences with past participle clauses: "Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention from thousands."
Use reflexive pronouns correctly: myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves — "She taught herself to code."
Form second conditional sentences to imagine unreal situations: "If I had more time, I would design a better poster."
Use comparatives with "the more / the less": "The more belligerent the tone, the less effective the message."
Form and use compound adjectives with hyphens: well-branded, fast-selling, multi-use, future-ready.
Change direct speech into reported speech: "She said that the offer was a concession."
Write a persuasive advertisement, a product review, or an analytical essay about advertising techniques.
Present your opinions about advertising clearly and persuasively in spoken English.
Use at least fifteen new vocabulary words from this unit confidently in speech and writing.
Think critically about how advertising shapes consumer behaviour, the ethics of persuasion, and the difference between information and manipulation.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and add them to your Word Wall.
Work through the grammar lessons in the Grammar file (Prepositions Preceding Nouns, Passive Voice, Participle Clauses, Reflexive Pronouns, Second Conditional, Comparatives, Compound Adjectives, Reported Speech).
Read the Story file, "Code Name: Bonanza," and answer the comprehension questions.
Come back to this lesson file and work through all twelve sections in order.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Before you read the passage below, make sure you know these important words. They appear throughout the unit. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary file or ask your AI tutor to explain it.

readings.passageScript:
aptitude — a natural ability to do something well.
adversary — a person or group that opposes or fights against another.
cunning — having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way.
momentum — the force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
bonanza — a situation or event that produces a large amount of something good.
concession — something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement.
accord — an agreement or harmony between people or groups.
insolent — showing a rude and disrespectful attitude.
pungent — having a strong smell or taste that is often sharp or biting.
demeanor — the way a person behaves or shows their feelings.
dainty — small and delicate in a way that is attractive.
belligerent — angry and ready to fight or argue.
intricate — very detailed and complicated, with many small parts.
indifferent — not caring about something; having no strong feelings about it.
impede — to slow down or stop something from happening.
emerge — to come out or appear from somewhere.
diminish — to make something smaller or less in amount, size, or importance.
chastise — to scold or criticize someone for doing something wrong.
dub — to give someone or something a name or title.
undulate — to move in waves or with a smooth, wavy motion.
billow — to rise and move in large waves or clouds.
lucrative — something that makes a lot of money or profit.
elusive — difficult to find, catch, or achieve.
bleak — cold, empty, and without hope or happiness.
bountiful — having a lot of something; plentiful or abundant.
attentive — paying close attention to something or someone.
desolate — feeling very sad and lonely; a place that is empty.
apprehensive — worried or anxious about something that might happen.
obsolete — no longer used or needed because something newer exists.
capacious — having a lot of space; able to hold a lot of things.
gusto — enjoyment or enthusiasm in doing something.
Read this passage carefully. Pay attention to the bold vocabulary words and the grammar structures used. After reading, you will answer comprehension questions and discuss the ideas.
Walk down any busy street in Mogadishu, Nairobi, or Addis Ababa and you will be surrounded by advertising. Posters shout about products on sale. Shop windows display items in stock. Radio jingles repeat catchy slogans. Sponsored posts fill your phone screen. Advertising is so pervasive that most people barely notice it anymore — yet its influence on our choices is enormous. Understanding how persuasion works is one of the most lucrative skills a young person can develop, not to make money from selling, but to protect yourself from being sold to without your knowledge.
Although advertising has existed for centuries, modern techniques are far more intricate than a simple market trader calling out prices. Today, advertisers use psychology, data analysis, and visual design to target specific audiences. A well-branded product does not just describe what it does — it creates a feeling. A sports drink advert does not simply say "this drink contains vitamins." Instead, it shows athletes running through rain, their muscles glistening, while dramatic music plays. The message is not about vitamins — it is about strength, ambition, and belonging to a winning team. The drink is dubbed a "performance booster" and sold at a premium.
One of the most powerful advertising techniques is the use of emotional appeal. Advertisers know that people make decisions based on feelings first and logic second. A charity advertisement might show a desolate landscape and a child with a sad expression to make viewers feel sympathy. A perfume advertisement might use soft lighting, elegant models, and a dainty bottle to create feelings of luxury and desire. Created by teams of designers and psychologists, these advertisements are carefully crafted to bypass your rational mind and speak directly to your emotions.

readings.title: 📖 About this story, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Genre: Realistic fiction with drama and social insight.
Theme: Resilience, persuasion, and ethical strategy in competitive environments.
Setting: Present-day Mogadishu, Somalia -- a modern tech high school with urban surroundings.
Moral: The more cunning the path, the less lasting the reward. Integrity and collaboration outpace deception.
The Unit 4 vocabulary words are in bold the first time they appear. When you meet one, say it out loud!
The morning air in Mogadishu was pungent with the smell of roasted coffee and car fumes as the courtyard of Sami Academy buzzed with students. Posters flapped against walls, advertising the annual "Innovation for Impact" challenge. The national competition brought together top student teams to pitch projects that combined technology with social good.
This year's theme: sustainable urban living.
Nadia eyed the poster as she passed, already knowing every detail. She had emerged as one of the most promising coders in school, known for her quiet focus and sharp aptitude in algorithms. But she was not competing alone.
"We have got five days," said Adam, her teammate. He had a bold demeanor and the annoying habit of tapping pens when thinking. "We can still win if we push."
"If we had more time," Nadia muttered, "we would perfect the drone's navigation."
Their project, a waste-sorting drone, was promising but glitchy. Sometimes it confused recyclable plastic for food waste. It was clever — maybe too clever — and the mechanics were intricate.
The school's tech lab, usually quiet, had turned into a battlefield. Tools and tablets were everywhere. Tension built with each hour.
Their biggest adversary was Team Horizon, led by Ibrahim — the kind of competitor who smiled sweetly while planting doubts in your mind.
"Nice drone," he said one afternoon, passing Nadia's table. "Shame it is not... reliable."
Nadia raised an eyebrow. "Our testing has just begun."
Ibrahim grinned. "So has ours. We have dubbed it 'The Beacon.'"
The name had already shown up on flyers around the campus. Well-branded. Fast-selling. Future-ready.
Created by a cunning team, The Beacon seemed flawless. Too flawless. Rumours swirled that they had copied elements from online builds.
When Nadia told Adam, he looked apprehensive.
"If they cheated, should we report it?"
Nadia shook her head. "Only if we have proof. Otherwise, it is just... noise."
On Wednesday, all teams gave a short demo. When it was Team Spark's turn, Adam stepped up, voice steady.
"Our drone sorts waste mid-air using object recognition. It runs on solar and can recharge at smart stations."
The drone rose. For a moment, all was smooth.
Then it wobbled, bumped into a shelf, and dropped a piece of cardboard meant to be sorted.
Nadia clenched her fists, blaming herself.
The judges nodded politely, saying little. Their eyes moved to Team Horizon, whose prototype hovered like a dream, sorting items into labelled bins with almost robotic grace.
Applause followed. One judge even whispered, "Now that is a bonanza of an idea."
Nadia felt the momentum diminish. Their odds shrank like morning mist.
Later that evening, Adam slammed a tablet down. "It is not fair. He is using tech we cannot trace. I bet it is not even original."
Nadia nodded, thinking. "Then let us beat them differently."
"The more belligerent we get, the less people listen. But the more we refine, the more we show who we are."
The team worked with new gusto, rewriting the sorting code and simplifying the drone's shell to improve balance. Nadia even wrote a script in passive voice for the final pitch:
"The drone is launched by solar timing. Waste is identified by AI and sorted automatically. Maintenance is managed remotely."
She trained herself to speak with calm authority. Adam changed his tone from fiery to firm. They practised until the pitch felt smooth.
Bright lights filled the auditorium. Parents, local reporters, and school heads sat in rows. Each team had ten minutes.

readings.title: 📖 About this story, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Team Horizon went first. Ibrahim's tone was slick. His smile wide.
"Our product, The Beacon, will transform waste into wealth."
He walked through their process, but avoided technical questions. One judge asked about source code.
"Proprietary," Ibrahim replied. "Protected for future commercial use."
Nadia caught a flash of confusion among the judges.
Then it was Team Spark's turn.
Created through weeks of testing, their new version was lighter, steadier, and clear in function.
Nadia spoke: "Built for local streets, the drone adapts to changes in light and wind. It was designed with community spaces in mind."
Their presentation included video clips, one where the drone dropped waste neatly into bins outside a park. The judges murmured approval.
Afterward, during Q&A, a judge asked, "How did you overcome your early bugs?"
"We evaluated each failure," Nadia answered. "And improved based on them. We learned from ourselves."
There was a long pause. Then applause.
Later, as everyone waited for results, Ibrahim approached.
"You are calm," he added. "I would be panicking if I were you."
"We did our best," she said, refusing to be drawn into his insolent games.
He stepped closer. "I could offer a concession. Maybe we say we collaborate next year. Share ideas."
Isaac narrowed his eyes. "We are not interested."
"Fine." Ibrahim walked away, his demeanor suddenly tense.
In third place: a drone that composted banana peels.
A roar followed. Nadia barely heard anything beyond the sound of Adam yelling in joy and hugging her.
The judges explained: "Team Spark impressed not only with innovation but with transparency and teamwork. They demonstrated growth, strategy, and an eye for ethical application."
Back at the lab, a breeze entered through open windows. Plans and sketches billowed off desks. Nadia caught one.
"Looks like we need a filing system," she said, laughing.
The room felt capacious, suddenly full of promise. Empty space had become opportunity.
Isaac was already scribbling ideas. "Maybe we create a multi-use drone next — well-branded and bright."
Nadia smiled. "We will need to study more. Better sensors. Better balance."
"If we had the funding," Adam mused, "we would launch a full pilot in the summer."
"If we had the funding," Nadia echoed, "we would create jobs too."
Outside, clouds undulated in waves across the sky. The competition was over, but momentum was building.
Nadia looked at her hands. They smelled faintly of soldering metal and soap. She had fought an uphill battle — not with belligerence, but with reason.
And that, she thought, was how to win with integrity.

grammar.title: Prepositions Preceding Nouns
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Prepositions are words that show the relationship between a noun and other parts of a sentence. When we say "prepositions preceding nouns," we mean prepositions that come before nouns to create common phrases. In advertising, these phrases appear constantly: items on sale, products in stock, offers valid for a limited time. Learning these fixed phrases will make your English sound natural and help you understand advertisements more easily.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Prepositions are words that show the relationship between a noun and other parts of a sentence. When we say "prepositions preceding nouns," we mean prepositions that come before nouns to create common phrases. In advertising, these phrases appear constantly: items on sale, products in stock, offers valid for a limited time. Learning these fixed phrases will make your English sound natural and help you understand advertisements more easily.
How to form and use them
Identify the noun you want to describe: sale, stock, time, demand, display.
Choose the right preposition: on, in, for, at, with.
Place the preposition before the noun: on + sale = on sale.
Use the phrase in a sentence: “The trainers are on sale until Friday.”
Common prepositional phrases in advertising:
on sale — “The shoes are on sale this week.”
in stock — “The toy is in stock at all our stores.”
for a limited time — “This offer is valid for a limited time.”
at a discount — “You can buy it at a discount today.”
on display — “The new collection is on display in the window.”
in demand — “These phones are in high demand.”
for commercial use — “The software is for commercial use only.”
with interest — “You can pay later with interest.”
Variations and exceptions
Some prepositional phrases are fixed and cannot be changed. You say "on sale" not "in sale."
The same noun can take different prepositions with different meanings: "in time" (not late) vs. "on time" (at the right moment).
Always learn prepositional phrases as complete units, not word by word.
Common mistakes
Mistake: “The item is in sale this week.” ✗  (wrong preposition)
Fix: “The item is on sale this week.” ✓
Mistake: “The book is table.” ✗  (preposition missing)
Fix: “The book is on the table.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of prepositions as position words. They help position nouns in relation to other words. When you see an advertising phrase, notice which preposition is used and learn the whole phrase together.
Practice 1
Fill in the blanks with the correct preposition (on, in, for, at):
The special offer is ______ sale until Sunday.
These headphones are ______ stock in all colours.
This discount is available ______ a limited time.
You can buy the jacket ______ a reduced price today.
The new phones are ______ high demand this month.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply prepositions preceding nouns in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Passive Voice (Present and Past)
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The passive voice focuses on the action or the receiver of the action, rather than on who performs it. This is important in advertising because companies want to emphasise the product, not themselves. It is also common in news reports, scientific writing, and formal descriptions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The passive voice focuses on the action or the receiver of the action, rather than on who performs it. This is important in advertising because companies want to emphasise the product, not themselves. It is also common in news reports, scientific writing, and formal descriptions.
How to form it
Present passive: subject + is/are + past participle.
“The product is sold in fifty countries.”
“The aisles are cleaned every morning.”
Past passive: subject + was/were + past participle.
“The product was dubbed a bonanza by the press.”
“The cake was eaten by the children.”
Optional agent: use "by" + doer. Sometimes the agent is left out:
With agent: “The meal was cooked by the chef.”
Without agent: “The homework is completed every evening.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “The book read by the students.” ✗  (missing form of to be)
Fix: “The book is read by the students.” ✓
Mistake: “The chef was cooked by the meal.” ✗  (subject and object reversed)
Fix: “The meal was cooked by the chef.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of the passive voice as a backwards sentence. The action is still happening, but the focus flips! Remember: Subject + form of 'to be' + past participle + (by + doer).
Practice 2
Rewrite these active sentences in the passive voice:
The choir sings the song every Sunday.
The students finished the homework yesterday.
The artist painted the picture last year.
The company designs the adverts.
The teacher read the story to the class.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply passive voice (present and past) in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Clauses Beginning with Past Participles
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Past participle clauses are short phrases that begin a sentence with a verb in its past participle form (typically ending in -ed, -en, -t, or -n). They add extra information and make your writing more sophisticated — they are especially popular in product descriptions and advertising copy.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Past participle clauses are short phrases that begin a sentence with a verb in its past participle form (typically ending in -ed, -en, -t, or -n). They add extra information and make your writing more sophisticated — they are especially popular in product descriptions and advertising copy.
How to form and use them
Choose a past participle: created, designed, built, written, excited, shocked.
Add detail: “Created by a cunning team...”
Add a comma and then the main clause: “Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention.”
Check the subject: the subject of the main clause must be the person or thing described by the participle.
More examples:
“Excited by the surprise party, she smiled widely.”
“Written in a hurry, the note was difficult to read.”
“Built by skilled workers, the bridge is strong and beautiful.”
“Designed for outdoor use, the speaker is waterproof.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “Created by a cunning team the ad drew attention.” ✗  (missing comma)
Fix: “Created by a cunning team, the ad drew attention.” ✓
Mistake: Using the wrong form of the verb. Make sure you use the correct past participle.
Memory tip
Think of past participle clauses as a quick snapshot before the main event — they set the scene in just a few words!
Practice 3
Complete the sentences using the correct past participle form:
______ (excite) by the performance, the audience cheered loudly.
______ (forget) his lunch, he had to borrow from a friend.
______ (prepare) for the test, she studied all night.
______ (design) by local engineers, the bridge won an award.
______ (shock) by the news, they sat in silence.

grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply clauses beginning with past participles in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Reflexive Pronouns
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
Reflexive pronouns are used when the subject and the object of a sentence are the same. They end in -self (singular) or -selves (plural). We also use them for emphasis — to show that someone did something alone or without help. In advertising, reflexive pronouns appear in emotional appeals: "Treat yourself to something special!"
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
Reflexive pronouns are used when the subject and the object of a sentence are the same. They end in -self (singular) or -selves (plural). We also use them for emphasis — to show that someone did something alone or without help. In advertising, reflexive pronouns appear in emotional appeals: "Treat yourself to something special!"
The reflexive pronouns
I → myself   |   you → yourself   |   he → himself   |   she → herself
it → itself   |   we → ourselves   |   you (plural) → yourselves   |   they → themselves
Examples:
“I enjoyed the concert myself.”
“She taught herself how to play the guitar.”
“We prepared the presentation ourselves.”
“He bought the gift for himself.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “I saw myself the teacher.” ✗  (subject and object are different)
Fix: “I saw the teacher.” ✓  (no reflexive needed here)
Memory tip
Reflexive pronouns have -self in them! They always point back to the subject. When you see "-self," think: "I am doing this to/for myself!"
Practice 4
Fill in the blanks with the correct reflexive pronoun:
I can design the poster by ______ .
She convinced ______ that the product was worth buying.
They built the drone all by ______ .
You should believe in ______ .
He prepared the advertising pitch by ______ .
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply reflexive pronouns in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Second Conditional
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The second conditional talks about imaginary or unreal situations in the present or future. It lets us dream, wish, and speculate. Advertisers use it to help customers imagine owning a product: "If you had this phone, you would never miss a moment." Understanding the second conditional helps you express wishes, give advice, and analyse persuasive language.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The second conditional talks about imaginary or unreal situations in the present or future. It lets us dream, wish, and speculate. Advertisers use it to help customers imagine owning a product: "If you had this phone, you would never miss a moment." Understanding the second conditional helps you express wishes, give advice, and analyse persuasive language.
How to form it
Structure: If + past simple, subject + would + base verb.
“If I had the aptitude, I would launch a lucrative brand.”
“If she were a superhero, she would save the world.”
“If they had more time, they would travel to space.”
Important: in the second conditional, use were (not "was") for all subjects in the if-clause. This is a special rule.
Common mistakes
Mistake: “If I was a bird, I would fly.” ✗  (use were for all subjects)
Fix: “If I were a bird, I would fly.” ✓
Mistake: “If I have a million dollars, I would travel.” ✗  (present tense in if-clause)
Fix: “If I had a million dollars, I would travel.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of the second conditional as a "dream" structure. You are imagining what could be! Always remember: "If I had" + "I would."
Practice 5
Complete the sentences using the second conditional:
If I ______ (be) the president, I ______ (make) the world a better place.
If my friend ______ (have) a car, we ______ (go) on a road trip.
If she ______ (know) the answer, she ______ (tell) us.
If we ______ (have) the funding, we ______ (launch) a pilot programme.
If he ______ (be) an advertiser, he ______ (create) only honest adverts.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply second conditional in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Comparatives with "the more" / "the less"
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
This structure shows how one thing affects another. As one quality increases, the other also increases (or decreases). It is powerful for analysing advertising because you can describe cause-and-effect relationships: "The more colourful the packaging, the more children want to buy it."
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
This structure shows how one thing affects another. As one quality increases, the other also increases (or decreases). It is powerful for analysing advertising because you can describe cause-and-effect relationships: "The more colourful the packaging, the more children want to buy it."
How to form it
Structure: The more/less + [adjective/verb], the more/less + [adjective/verb].
“The more you practise, the better you get.”
“The less sugar you eat, the healthier you feel.”
“The more belligerent the tone, the less effective the message.”
“The more exciting the story, the more people want to read it.”
Common mistakes
Mistake: “More you study, better you get.” ✗  (missing "the")
Fix: “The more you study, the better you get.” ✓
Memory tip
Think of it as a seesaw: as one side goes up, the other goes up too (or down). "The more" = this side rises. "The more/less" = the other side follows.
Practice 6
Complete the sentences:
The ______ you read, the ______ you learn.
The ______ you exercise, the ______ your body feels.
The ______ noise there is, the ______ we can hear each other.
The ______ creative the advert, the ______ people remember it.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply comparatives with "the more" / "the less" in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

writing.title: Writing 1:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
In this section, you will choose one of three writing projects. Each project uses the vocabulary, grammar, and ideas from this unit. The goal is to practise extended writing while thinking critically about advertising and persuasion.
✍️ Writing rules to remember
Start every sentence with a capital letter. End with a full stop, question mark, or exclamation mark.
Use vocabulary from this unit: cunning, lucrative, momentum, bonanza, diminish, etc.
Include at least two prepositional phrases (on sale, in stock, for a limited time), one passive voice sentence, one reflexive pronoun, and one second conditional sentence.
Read your work out loud, or ask your AI tutor to check it, before you finish.
Option A: Create a Persuasive Advertisement
You have been hired to create an advertisement for a new product. The product can be anything you choose: a gadget, a food product, a piece of clothing, or a service. Write a full-page advertisement (about 250–300 words) that would convince people to buy it.
Your advertisement should include:
A catchy name and slogan for the product. Use a compound adjective: "Our future-ready device changes everything."
An emotional appeal: how will the product make the customer feel? Use vivid description.
A logical appeal: give at least one statistic or fact about the product's quality or performance.

activities.title: Activities 1: Advert Analysis
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Find a real advertisement — from a newspaper, a website, a poster, or a screenshot from social media. Stick it in your notebook or describe it in detail. Then answer:
What product or service is being advertised?
Who is the target audience (children, teenagers, adults, parents)?
What persuasive technique(s) does it use: emotional appeal, logical appeal, social proof, or celebrity endorsement?
Does it use any prepositional phrases like on sale, in stock, or for a limited time?
Does it use any compound adjectives like well-branded or fast-selling?
Is the advert honest? Does it make any claims that seem exaggerated or misleading?
How effective do you think the advert is? Rate it out of 10 and explain your rating.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Before and After
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Many products that were once considered lucrative have become obsolete. Think of three examples of products or technologies that are no longer widely used (for example: cassette tapes, fax machines, film cameras). For each one:
Write one sentence explaining what the product was and why it was popular. Use passive voice: "Cassette tapes were used by millions of people to listen to music."
Write one sentence explaining why it became obsolete. Use a comparative: "The more digital music improved, the less people needed cassettes."
Write one sentence imagining what would happen if it came back. Use the second conditional: "If cassette tapes were reintroduced today, young people would probably find them charming but impractical."
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Create a Brand Identity
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Imagine you are starting a small business in your local area (a bakery, a tutoring service, a clothing shop, a tech repair business). Create a complete brand identity:
Business name (use a compound adjective if possible: "Quick-Fix Tech Repairs").
Slogan (a short, memorable phrase: "Well-branded solutions for well-lived lives").
Logo description (you do not need to draw it — describe what it would look like in three to four sentences).
Target audience (who are your customers and why?).
A short advertising paragraph (five to six sentences) using at least three vocabulary words, one passive voice sentence, and one prepositional phrase.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Vocabulary Crossword Clues
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write crossword-style clues for ten vocabulary words from this unit. Your clue should be a definition or a sentence with a blank. Exchange your clues with a classmate or give them to your AI tutor to solve.
Example:
Across 1: A situation that produces a large amount of something good (7 letters) → bonanza
Down 2: Angry and ready to fight or argue (11 letters) → belligerent
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: The Ethics Challenge
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Read these three scenarios and decide whether the advertising described is ethical (honest and fair) or unethical (misleading or harmful). Write two to three sentences for each, explaining your decision and using vocabulary from this unit.
A fast-food company shows happy, healthy-looking children eating their meals in an advert, but the meals contain very high levels of sugar and fat.
A mobile phone company says their new phone has "the longest battery life of any phone on sale," and this claim is backed up by independent testing.
A social media influencer praises a skin cream and says it "changed my life," but does not tell viewers that the company paid her to say this.
Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you help me analyse a real advertisement I found?
Can you check my brand identity writing for grammar and vocabulary?
Can you give me more ethical scenarios to discuss about advertising?
Vocabulary Reinforcement
By now you have met the Unit 4 vocabulary words in the passage, the story, the grammar exercises, and the activities. This section gives you extra practice to make sure the words are firmly in your memory.

quizzes.question: What does 'aptitude' mean?
quizzes.options: A natural ability to do something well. | A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good. | Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way. | The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A natural ability to do something well.
quizzes.explanation: She has a great aptitude for learning new languages quickly.

quizzes.question: What does 'adversary' mean?
quizzes.options: A person or group that opposes or fights against another. | A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good. | Something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement. | The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person or group that opposes or fights against another.
quizzes.explanation: In the competition, each team tried to outsmart their adversary to win.

quizzes.question: What does 'cunning' mean?
quizzes.options: A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good. | An agreement or harmony between people or groups. | Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way. | Something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way.
quizzes.explanation: The cunning fox tricked the farmer and stole some chickens.

quizzes.question: What does 'momentum' mean?
quizzes.options: An agreement or harmony between people or groups. | Showing a rude and disrespectful attitude. | Something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement. | The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
quizzes.explanation: The car gained momentum as it rolled down the hill.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A natural ability to do something well. She has a great aptitude for learning new languages quickly.
A person or group that opposes or fights against another. In the competition, each team tried to outsmart their adversary to win.
Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way. The cunning fox tricked the farmer and stole some chickens.
The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster. The car gained momentum as it rolled down the hill.
A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good. The school fair was a bonanza of fun activities and delicious food.
Something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement. After a long discussion, they made a concession and agreed to meet halfway.
An agreement or harmony between people or groups. The students reached an accord about how to work together on the project.
Showing a rude and disrespectful attitude. The insolent student talked back to the teacher in class.
Having a strong smell or taste that is often sharp or biting. The pungent smell of garlic filled the kitchen as my mother cooked dinner.
The way a person behaves or shows their feelings. Her calm demeanor made everyone feel relaxed during the meeting.
